title: How to Read IRS Transcripts: The Complete Tax Pro Guide
description: A complete guide to reading IRS transcripts: the four transcript types, how to request them, what each section means, the transaction codes that matter, the red flags to catch, and how to automate interpretation.
content:
Why IRS Transcripts Matter

An IRS transcript is the most reliable record of what the IRS actually has on file for a taxpayer. It tells you what was filed, what was assessed, what was paid, what was refunded, what was held, and what the IRS is doing right now. Reading transcripts well is one of the highest-leverage skills in tax practice — it is the difference between guessing and knowing.

This guide walks through the four transcript types, how to request each one, what every major section means, the transaction codes you have to recognize on sight, the red flags that demand action, and how to turn 20 minutes of manual interpretation into a 10-second automated report.

The Four Transcript Types

The IRS publishes four transcripts for individual taxpayers. Each is a different lens on the same account, and using the wrong one is the most common reason a transcript review goes sideways.

TranscriptWhat it showsUse it for
Account TranscriptEvery transaction posted to the account: assessments, payments, penalties, interest, refunds, holds, notices, audit activity.Collections, exam, refund delays, balance-due work, penalty abatement, anything timing-sensitive.
Return TranscriptThe return as originally filed, line by line. No amendments, no IRS adjustments.Mortgage and loan verification, FAFSA, prior-year reconstruction, confirming what the taxpayer reported.
Record of AccountAccount Transcript and Return Transcript combined into one document.When you need both the original return and the full transaction history in a single place.
Wage & Income TranscriptEvery information return reported to the IRS under the SSN: W-2, 1099, 1098, K-1, SSA, etc.Reconstructing unfiled returns, catching missing income, responding to CP2000 notices.

Rule of thumb: the Account Transcript answers what the IRS is doing, the Return Transcript answers what the taxpayer said, and the Wage & Income Transcript answers what third parties reported. Most resolution work starts with the Account Transcript and pulls the others as needed.

How to Request a Transcript

There are four practical ways to obtain transcripts as a tax professional:

  • e-Services Transcript Delivery System (TDS). Requires a CAF number and a valid Form 2848 or 8821 on file. This is the fastest method for practitioners and returns transcripts in minutes.
  • Practitioner Priority Service (PPS). Call 866-860-4259 with the POA on hand. PPS will fax transcripts to a secure eFax line. Use this when TDS is down or the CAF has not posted yet.
  • IRS.gov client self-service. The taxpayer logs in to their IRS Online Account and downloads the PDF directly. Useful when there is no POA in place.
  • Form 4506-T. Mail-in request. Slow (10+ business days) and only used when nothing else is available.

Always pull at least two years of Account Transcripts on a new resolution case. Patterns matter, and a single year often hides a multi-year story.

Reading the Account Transcript Section by Section

An Account Transcript has a predictable structure. Read it in this order and you will not miss anything.

  1. Header. Confirm SSN, name control, tax period, and form number. Wrong period is the #1 source of transcript misreads.
  2. Account Balance. The number at the top is as of the cycle date — not today. Interest and penalties continue to accrue.
  3. Accrued Interest and Accrued Penalty. These are projected forward to the next cycle date and are usually understated for collections planning.
  4. Return Filing Section. Look for TC 150 (return filed and tax assessed) and the cycle code. The cycle code tells you the week the return posted.
  5. Transaction Section. The chronological ledger. This is where most of the work happens. Read it top to bottom and build a timeline.
  6. Civil Penalty / Examination Section. If present, this is where audit and penalty activity lives.

The Transaction Codes Every Tax Pro Must Recognize

There are over 200 transaction codes. You only need to know about 30 of them cold. These are the ones that drive almost every resolution decision.

CodeMeaningWhy it matters
150Return filed and tax assessedAnchors the timeline. Everything dates from here.
766 / 768Refundable credit / EIC appliedReduces tax. Confirms credits posted as expected.
806W-2 / 1099 withholding creditConfirms reported withholding was accepted.
570Additional account action pending (refund freeze)Refund is held. Investigate before promising the client anything.
571 / 572Resolved 570Hold released. Refund typically issues in 1–2 cycles.
971Notice issuedA letter went out. Pair with the action code to identify which one.
846Refund issuedThe refund actually left the IRS. Date is the issue date.
290 / 291Additional / abated tax assessmentIRS adjusted the return. Compare against the original 150.
420 / 424Examination indicatorThe return is in or selected for audit.
480 / 482Offer in Compromise pending / acceptedTracks OIC status independently of the offer specialist.
520 / 521 / 522Litigation / appeals freezeCollections is paused. Useful in CDP and innocent-spouse work.
530Currently Not CollectibleCNC status posted. Confirm the closing code.
582 / 583Lien filed / releasedConfirms NFTL activity.
971 + 977Amended return received1040-X is in the pipeline. Watch for 290/291 to follow.

Red Flags You Cannot Miss

  • TC 570 with no matching 571. Refund is frozen. Do not promise the client a refund date.
  • TC 420 or 424 in the current year. Audit is open or pending. Stop and pull the exam history.
  • TC 922 or TC 925. Underreporter / CP2000 activity. Pull the Wage & Income Transcript immediately.
  • TC 971 with action code 069 / 121 / 124. Identity verification, fraud, or substitute-for-return activity.
  • TC 599 with no TC 150. IRS satisfied a non-filer notice without a return actually being assessed — usually means SFR is coming.
  • Cycle date older than 6 weeks with no activity. The return is stuck. Call PPS.
  • TC 706 / 826. Refund offset to a prior year or another agency. The client will not see the money.

A Repeatable Reading Workflow

  1. Confirm the period and form number in the header.
  2. Note the account balance and the as-of date.
  3. Find TC 150 and write down the cycle code.
  4. Build a timeline from TC 150 forward, code by code.
  5. Flag every freeze, hold, and notice. Cross-reference 971s with their action codes.
  6. Compare TC 290 / 291 against the original assessment.
  7. If a refund is expected, locate TC 846 — if it is missing, find what is blocking it.
  8. Write a one-paragraph plain-English summary the client can read in 30 seconds.
  9. Document what you checked and what you concluded. The next preparer will thank you.

Done well, this takes 15 to 25 minutes per transcript. Done badly, it produces vague answers and unhappy clients.

Automating Transcript Interpretation

Most of the work above is mechanical: identify codes, build the timeline, flag the freezes, write the summary. Transcript Tax Monitor Pro does the mechanical part in under ten seconds. Upload an Account Transcript PDF and you get the timeline, every code translated into plain English, the red flags called out, and a client-ready narrative — all generated locally in your browser, with no taxpayer data ever leaving your machine.

The point is not to replace judgment. It is to skip the 20 minutes of code lookups and timeline-building so the judgment work starts immediately.
